coredata.dc:description: Recurrent infection of chronic wounds remains a major clinical challenge. Recently, the hydrogel antibacterial materials have attracted extensive attention for preventing infection in wound healing. In this study, a hybrid hydrogel made of polyvinyl alcohol - iodine (PAI), sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), and carbamino quantum dot (CQDs) was prepared by the cross-linking of hydrogen bonds, named as polyvinyl alcohol‑iodine/sodium carboxymethyl cellulose/carbon quantum dots (PAI/CMC/CQDs). The composite hydrogels exhibited the outstanding photothermal conversion efficiency with near infrared (NIR) light irradiation, and the high antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (E. coli). Meanwhile, the elevated temperature of the composite hydrogels up to ∼45 °C was able to stimulate the migration of epidermal cell to accelerate skin repair. Given that PAI and CQDs could respond to different pH values (5–8), the real-time would pH information was provided by the visible light and fluorescent light dual monitoring system by naked eye. Moreover, the visible-fluorescent images could be collected and transformed into RGB signals to quantify the would pH levels, avoiding secondary injuries caused by frequent dressing changes. PAI/CMC/CQDs was demonstrated the significant therapeutic effect on chronic wounds by eliminating bacterial infections and promoting skin repair under the smart RGB monitoring system.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CQDs and hydrogels. (a) Schematic illustration of the pH-responsive fluorescent CQDs for hydrogel dressings to detect chronic wounds. (b) The XPS full scan survey spectra of CQDs. (c) XRD patterns of PVA, PVA/CMC, PAI/CMC, and PAI/CMC/CQDs. (d) The swelling ratio of different content of CMC in hydrogels. (e) SEM images of PVA and PAI/CMC/CQDs. (f) EDS spectrum images of C, O, I, Na, and N element. Data were shown as the mean ± SD (n = 3).

Assessment the smartphone-guided real-time pH monitoring function of PAI/CAM/CQDs. (a) The fluorescence of CQDs in aqueous solution of different pH values. (b) The excitation wavelengths of CQDs at different pH values. (c) The hydrogels photographs and RGB images at different pH values under UV and (d) visible light exposure. (e) The R, G, B and the fitting curves of hydrogels as a function of pH values under visible and (f) ultraviolet light. Data were shown as the mean ± SD (n = 3).

The mechanical robustness and swelling properties of the hydrogels. (a-d) Images of PAI/CMC/CQDs samples in stretchable, bendable, twistable forms. (e) Compression stress-strain curves of PVA, PVA/CMC, PAI/CMC, PAI/CMC/CQDs. (f) Cyclic compressive load-unload curves of PAI/CMC/CQDs. (g) Tensile stress-strain curves of PVA, PVA/CMC, PAI/CMC, PAI/CMC/CQDs. (h) Cyclic tensile load-unload curves of PAI/CMC/CQDs. (i)The energy storage modulus (G′) and loss modulus (G″) of PVA, PVA/CMC, PAI/CMC, PAI/CMC/CQDs. (j) The swelling ratio of the hydrogels in different pH. Data were shown as the mean ± SD (n = 3).

Photothermal performance of PAI/CMC/CQDs. (a) Infrared thermal images of PAI/CMC/CQDs. (b) The corresponding time dependent temperature increase curves of PAI/CMC/CQDs hydrogel at different laser densities. (c) Plot of cooling time versus -ln(θ). (d) Irradiation cycle of PAI/CMC/CQDs with 808 nm laser (1 W/cm2). (e) The I2 release curves on PAI/CMC/CQDs hydrogel with or without NIR.

The antibacterial activity and the cell compatibility of PAI/CMC/CQDs in vitro. (a) The images of bacteria colony forming units after culture with hydrogel extracts for 24 h. (b) Representative FESEM images of bacteria after immersing into hydrogel extracts for 1 h. (c) The bacterical viability of E.coli. and (d) S.aureus. (e) The cell viability and (f) the fluorescence intensity of L929 cells cultivated with PVA/CMC, PAI/CMC, PAI/CMC/CQDs, and PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR hydrogel. (g) The representative images of the transwell migration assay of HUVECs and L929 cells cultivated with PVA/CMC, PAI/CMC, PAI/CMC/CQDs, and PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR. Data were shown as the mean ± SD (n = 3). **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001.

The wound healing after the treatment of PVA/CMC, PAI/CMC, PAI/CMC/CQDs, and PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR. (a) Timeline of the wound treatment process. (b) Thermal images of temperature evolution on mice treated with PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR. (c) Representative photographs of skin wound treated with PVA/CMC, PAI/CMC, PAI/CMC/CQDs, and PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR from day 0 to day 14. Scale bar: 5 mm. (d) Photographs of wound-bed closure after various treatments during 14 days: (1) Control, (2) PVA/CMC, (3) PAI/CMC, (4) PAI/CMC/CQDs, and (5) PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR. (e) Evolution of wound area on day 3, 7, 10 and 14. (f) H&E and Masson staining of wound tissues on day 14 with treatments of PVA/CMC, PAI/CMC, PAI/CMC/CQDs, and PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR. Scale bar: 100 μm. Data were shown as the mean ± SD (n = 5).

Real-time wound pH monitoring of PAI/CMC/CQDs (a) Photographs of the color changes of the hydrogel covering the trauma surface over time (under visible light or UV light). (b) The pH variation of mice wound model between the real and the testing value. Data were shown as the mean ± SD (n = 3).

(a) Schematic illustration of PAI/CMC/CQDs preparation. (b) Schematic illustration of pH real-time monitoring and accelerated wound healing by the integrated photo-inspired antibacterial PAI/CMC/CQDs dressings.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recurrent infection of chronic wounds remains a major clinical challenge. Recently, the hydrogel antibacterial materials have attracted extensive attention for preventing infection in wound healing. In this study, a hybrid hydrogel made of polyvinyl alcohol - iodine (PAI), sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), and carbamino quantum dot (CQDs) was prepared by the cross-linking of hydrogen bonds, named as polyvinyl alcohol‑iodine/sodium carboxymethyl cellulose/carbon quantum dots (PAI/CMC/CQDs). The composite hydrogels exhibited the outstanding photothermal conversion efficiency with near infrared (NIR) light irradiation, and the high antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (E. coli). Meanwhile, the elevated temperature of the composite hydrogels up to ∼45 °C was able to stimulate the migration of epidermal cell to accelerate skin repair. Given that PAI and CQDs could respond to different pH values (5–8), the real-time would pH information was provided by the visible light and fluorescent light dual monitoring system by naked eye. Moreover, the visible-fluorescent images could be collected and transformed into RGB signals to quantify the would pH levels, avoiding secondary injuries caused by frequent dressing changes. PAI/CMC/CQDs was demonstrated the significant therapeutic effect on chronic wounds by eliminating bacterial infections and promoting skin repair under the smart RGB monitoring system.

ce:section.ce:para: Chronic wounds continue to be a serious burden for the worldwide healthcare system, with a high prevalence, costly, and time-consuming treatment methods [1–4]. To date, there are several materials have been employed for promoting wound healing [5,6], such as sponges [7], gauze [8], and hydrogels [9]. In recent years, the hydrogels had gradually been widely used in soft tissue repair due to their excellent biocompatibility, moisturizing properties, and unique mechanical properties [10–12]. However, frequent debridement and replacing of hydrogel dressings based on experience may damage fresh skin tissue and increase the risk of bacterial infection in clinical care [13,14]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop the smart hydrogel dressings with infection prevention and wound monitoring functions [15,16].

As one of the key factors of wound tissues, the pH value of the wound microenvironment is closely related to wound diagnosis and treatment [17]. Commonly, the wound tissues own a higher pH value (7–8) compared with the normal skin tissues (4–6) and vary with chronic wound status, which can accurately reflect the stages of wound healing [18]. Thus, monitoring the pH change of the wound microenvironment can help identify difficult-to-heal wounds and adjust treatment plans in a timely manner [19]. Based on this, researchers created the pH-responsive dressings as a fast diagnostic tool for the treatment of chronic wounds [20,21]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a fluorescent nanomaterial have the advantages of excellent biocompatibility, stable photoluminescence, and tunable luminescence emission [22–24]. Some CQDs were sensitive and responsive to pH changes in the range of 5–8 via fluorescence and visual colorimetric changes, which would be a right candidate for biosensor to real-time monitor of wound healing [25–27].

Photothermal antibacterial (PTA) is an advanced therapeutic method for treating recurrent infected wounds, which combines the photothermal agents (PTAs) and corresponding light irradiation. PTAs can convert light energy into heat under light irradiation, resulting in bacterial membrane rupture and protein denaturation, which in turn kills the bacteria [28–31]. However, the additional introduction of PTAs would increase the risk of systemic toxicity [32] and the uncontrollable temperature rise might damage to normal tissues skin, these potential side effects would limit the clinical use of PTA [33]. The novel photo-inspired antimicrobial hydrogel (PAI) was compounded by polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) with excellent biocompatibility [34] and the broad-spectrum antimicrobial agents of iodophor (KI-I2) [35]. PAI exhibited the efficient photothermal antibacterial ability by controlling the release of I2 in a safe temperature range (less than the skin burn threshold of 45 °C), which could avoid secondary damage to wounds caused by the excessive temperature [36]. More importantly, it has been confirmed that an appropriate temperature elevation would enhance cell migration and aids in wound healing [37,38]. Additionally, the color responsiveness of PAI to pH changes facilitates synergistic monitoring for chronic wound repair.

Herein, we developed a multifunctional hydrogel material for monitoring and promoting would healing with hemostasis, infection prevention, photothermal antibacterial, pH real-time monitoring, and skin restoration capabilities. Carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) hydrogel is an ideal base material for wound dressings with high swelling, good flexibility, and self-cleaning properties [39,40]. CMC was selected as the network backbone, and combined with the photothermal antibacterial hydrogel of PAI, doped with the fluorescent material of CQDs, to construct a hydrogel dressing (PAI/CMC/CQDs) to protect the wound, reduce infection，and accelerate healing process. Under NIR illumination, I2 was gradually released from PAI/CMC/CQDs to protect wound from the further bacterial infection, and the increased temperature accelerated cell migration to promote wound healing. Benefit from the color responsiveness of PAI and CQDs to pH of wound tissues in visible light and UV light, the smartphone was utilized to collect and convert the pH sensing images to the red, green, and blue (RGB) signals to visually capture the real-time pH values during the wound healing process. As a result, the integrated photo-induced antibacterial system provided an ingenious method for real-time monitoring and quick diagnosis in wound healing, which has enormous potential to realize clinical translation (Scheme 1 ).

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA-1799, 98 % ∼ 99 %) was obtained from Macklin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Carboxymethyl cellulose sodium (CMC, USP), Iodine (I2, 99.8 %) and were purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥ 96 %), lsocyanate (CHNO, 98 %), urea (CO(NH₂)₂, 99 %), and citric acid (C6H8O7, 98 %) were purchased from China National Medicines Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China，CP). S. aureus (BNCC 186335) and E. coli (BNCC 336953) were obtained from Beina Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) was obtained from Shanghai Beyotime Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) and PBS were purchased from Wuhan servicebio Technology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was obtained from Australia Gibco Technology Co., Ltd. (Australia). All other chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA) and used without further purification.

Firstly, anhydrous citric acid (0.108 g), NaOH (0.05 g), and urea (0.216 g) were ultrasonic dissolved in a mixture solution of ethanol (20 mL) and n-butyl isocyanate (5 mL). Then, the transparent solution was placed sequentially into autoclaves and oven, and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the crude products were purified by dialysis bag (1000 Da) for three days and then freeze-dried, diluted in 10 mL of DI water to obtain purified dark brown CQDs.

To synthesize PAI hydrogel, KI-I2 solution (the mixture of 20 mg of I2 and 200 mg of KI in 10 mL of water) and PVA aqueous solution (10 %, w/v) were pre-prepared, respectively. Subsequently, 1 mL of CMC aqueous solution (1 %, w/v) was added into the mixing solution of 9 mL of 10 % PVA and 2 mL of KI-I2. CQDs was then loaded into the hydrogel by the one-step-added method and stirred for 2 h until uniformly mixed. After standing for defoaming completely, the mixed solution was poured into the mold, followed by freezing at −20 °C for 12 h, and then standing at room temperature for 5 h with three cycles to obtain the fluorescent hydrogel of PAI/CMC/CQDs.

The microstructure of the as-prepared CQDs was investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-2100 plus, Japan). The sample was dropped onto a copper mesh, dried in air overnight, installed, and observed. The crystal structure of the synthesized CQDs was examined by X-ray diffraction (DB ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany). The chemical elements and bonding-state characterization of the CQDs were observed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA).

The fluorescence characteristics of the hydrogels were determined by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (LS-55; Perkin-Elmer, Waltham, MA, USA). The microstructure of hydrogels was studied using scanning electron microscope (SEM) (SIGMA 500, Opton Optical, Beijing China) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (SIGMA 500, Opton Optical, Beijing China). Before SEM and FESEM, the hydrogels were freeze-dried and sprayed with gold. Furthermore, the tensile, compression cyclic stretching, and cyclic compression tests on the hydrogels were performed at room temperature on a tensile machine (3360, Instron, 10 kN load cell, Norwood, MA, USA). The hydrogel samples (40 × 4 × 2 mm3) were prepared for single time tensile testing. The hydrogel samples (50 × 10 × 5 mm3) were created for cyclic tensile testing at a stretching rate of 50 mm/min. Additionally, a cylindrical shape (15 mm height, 20 mm diameter) was used for the compression tests of hydrogel. The energy storage modulus and loss modulus of samples were assessed through a Tainstruments Discovery hybrid rheometer (TA DHR-2). The hydrogel materials (cylinders of 25 mm diameter and 2 mm thickness) were subjected to frequency sweep tests in the frequency range of 0.1–100 rad/s at a fixed strain of 0.5 %.

To study the photothermal conversion efficiency of the materials, the hydrogel samples (0.3 mL) were constantly exposed to 808 nm NIR laser (1.0 W/cm2) for 10 min. At the same time, a FLIRE 60 infrared thermal imager was used to monitor the temperature changes and collect the thermal pictures. To investigate I2 release from PAI/CMC/CQDs under NIR light, the hydrogels were immersed in PBS solution (1.5 mL, pH = 7.4) and irradiated under the 808 nm NIR laser (1.0 W/cm2) for 10 min. Simultaneously, the hydrogel without laser irradiation was used as a control.

For the degradation test in vitro, the hydrogel samples with same mass were immersed in PBS solution at 37 °C (25 mL, pH = 7.4) [41] and divided into four groups: PAI/CMC/CQDs, PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR, PAI/CMC/CQDs + enzyme, PAI/CMC/CQDs + enzyme + NIR. After 5 days, the hydrogel samples were taken out, washed with distilled water, and then frozen. The weight remaining ratio of hydrogels was calculated by the following equation [42]:
The weight remaining ratio =
W t
/
W 0
× 100 %
where W0 and Wt are the weights of the samples before and after degradation, respectively.

In vitro hemostasis test was conducted according to the literature [43]. The whole blood of mouse was used to investigate the hemostatic properties of PAI/CMC/CQDs hydrogel. PAI/CMC/CQDs and PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR was mixed with 400 μL of whole blood in vials, respectively. Then, the vials were converted to observe whether the samples could sustain their weight.

E. coli and S. aureus were used to evaluate antibacterial activity of PAI/CMC/CQDs hydrogels in vitro. In a nutshell, 20 μL of the hydrogel was transferred into a tube along with PBS, PVA/CMC, PAI/CMC, PAI/CMC/CQDs. The hydrogel surfaces were then treated with 100 μL of S. aureus or E. coli bacterial solution (107 CFU/mL) before being exposed to NIR laser (808 nm, 1.0 W/cm2) radiation for 10 min. The hydrogels without NIR served as the blank control group. All groups were co-incubated with bacteria for 1 h. Subsequently, 10 μL of PBS was added to each hole, and then 25 μL of the diluted medium was plated on agar plates. After 10 h of incubation in the incubator, the bacteria were photographed and counted.

Antibiotic-treated bacteria were observed by SEM. The bacterial liquid co-incubated with different materials were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min and treated with 4 % paraformaldehyde overnight. The fixed samples were washed in PBS and dehydrated with 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 % ethanol for 10 min. Later, 10 μL of bacterial samples were placed on silicon for SEM characterization.

Mouse-derived fibroblasts (L929) cells and human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) were provided from China Centre for Type Culture Collection (Wuhan, China). L929 cells and HUVECs were cultured in DMEM (High Glucose) medium containing 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin-streptomysin (P/S) under 5 % CO2 at 37 °C.

HUVECs (5 × 103 cells/well) and L929 cells (5 × 103 cells/well) were seeded in 24-well plates and incubated overnight. Then, the medium was replaced with fresh medium containing different leaching solutions. For heat treatment group, mild heat stimulation was applied by incubating cells at 40 °C for 15 min. After 1, 3, and 5 days, the viabilities of different cells were determined by CCK-8 assay. To visually observe the viability of cells, the cells after incubation for 5 days were stained with PI (red fluorescence to mark dead cells) and calcein AM (green fluorescence to mark live cells) for 30 min and observed under a fluorescence inverted microscope.

The hemolysis rates of hydrogels were tested in vitro. First, 1 g of hydrogel samples (PVA/CMC, PAI/CMC, PAI/CMC/CQDs, and PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR) were added to 1 mL of PBS at 37 °C. Then, 100 μL of erythrocyte stock dispersions and 900 μL sample suspensions were added into the EP tubes and placed in a shaker for 12 h (100 rpm/ min, 37 °C). Then, the absorbances of the supernatant were determined at 540 nm using a microplate reader. PBS and distilled water were served as the negative control and positive control, respectively. The hemolysis rate (%) was calculated by the following equation:
Hemolysis rate
%
=
D s
−
D n
/
D w
−
D n
× 100 %
where Ds, Dn, and Dw represented the absorbance of the samples, PBS, and distilled water, respectively.

Transwell systems were used to investigate the effect of different hydrogels on cell migration. In brief, the leaching solution of different hydrogels were placed in bottom of 24-well plate with complete medium, and then L929 cells and HUVECs were introduced into the Transwell inserts at a concentration of 1 × 104 cells per well. For heat treatment group, mild heat stimulation was applied by incubating cells at 40 °C for 15 min. After 24 h incubation, crystal violet staining was performed to qualitatively observe the migrating cells underside.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo wound healing effect study
ce:section.ce:para:
A total of 25 experimental mice (BALB/c, male, 6 weeks old) were randomly divided into 5 groups. After anesthetized with 4 % chloral hydrate (0.1 mL/10 g body weight), the skin defect model, a full thickness excision with a diameter of 0.8 cm was established on the back of each mouse with sutures around fixed with the silicone ring. Then, the mice were treated with PVA/CMC, PAI/CMC, PAI/CMC/CQDS, or PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR (hydrogels with 8 mm in diameter and 4 mm in thickness), Control mice were not treated. At day 0, 3, 7, 10 and 14 after surgery, the wounds were photographed and the wound area was measured by using Image J. software.
Wound closure area
%
=
W 0
−
W t
/
W 0
× 100 %
where W0 represents the initial wound area on day 0 and Wt represents the wound area at the indicated time.
At last, the wound tissues containing the wound bed and surrounding skin were collected and fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and Masson trichrome staining.

All the animal experiments were carried out followed by current guidelines for the care of laboratory animals and were approved by the Experimental Animal Welfare Ethic Committee of Wuhan University (ZN2022051).
The data were reported as mean ± SD. The significant difference (p value) between two groups was calculated through two-tailed Student's t-test of Excel software. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001.

Fluorescent CQDs were synthesized in response to the pH of trauma microenvironment (Fig. 1a). Firstly, the carbamino quantum dots were synthesized utilizing a hydrothermal process and several dopants as source ingredients. The morphology of CQDs was observed under HR-TEM. As showed in HR-TEM image (Fig. S1a), CQDs were spherical and evenly dispersed with the narrow distribution from 4.50 to 6.50 nm and an average diameter of 5.96 ± 0.39 nm. Additionally, XRD and XPS were employed to analyze the functional groups and chemical composition of CQDs. As displayed in Fig. S1b, the XRD pattern of CQDs showed a sharp intense peak positioned at 2θ of 26.48°, representing the conjugate sp2 carbon structure associated with the aromatic ring structure [44]. Furthermore, we discovered four different XPS peaks at 531, 399, and 284 eV, which correspond to the atoms O, N, and C in CQDS, respectively (The presence K and Br were from carrier KBr) (Fig. 1b). The O1s spectra demonstrated that the O element in CQDs was predominantly present as C-OH/C-O-C and CO [45] (Fig. S2a), the N1s spectrum suggested that the N element in CQDs might occur largely as pyridine-like N, ON, and CN [46] (Fig. S2b), and the C1s spectrum indicated that the C component in CQDs was usually found in the forms CO and C-OH/C-O-C [47] (Fig. S2c). Overall, the findings presented above demonstrated the successful synthesis of N-doped CQDs [48].

Repeated infections and frequent dressing changes that damage the skin are the main reasons for the difficulty in wound healing [49]. Therefore, the advanced PAI/CMC/CQDs hydrogel dressings were developed by one pot method to obtain the smart materials that could monitor and assist in wound healing. In briefly, the photothermal antibacterial hydrogel of PAI was constructed by mixing KI-I2 solution into PVA hydrogel at pH = 7.4. Then, PAI/CMC was formed through hydrogen bond cross-linking between PAI and CMC, which made the structure of hydrogel porous and stable. Finally, the CQDs with pH-responsive fluorescence were incorporated into the system to obtained the integrated photothermal induced wound healing dressings. The dressing hydrogels with the pores structure have good moisturizing properties and also keep the CQDs existing in the dress to stabilize the fluorescence properties of the hydrogel.

Firstly, FTIR and XRD were used to analyze the hydrogel's functional group and crystal structure. As shown in Fig. S3, PVA exhibited a distinctive band at 1400 cm−1 and 3410 cm−1 due to the hydroxyl group, whereas the band in the area 1000–1110 cm−1 was related to -OH bending and stretching of -C-O. In the infrared spectrogram of PVA/CMC, a wider peak from the stretching vibration of the -OH portion close to 3410 cm−1 was detected, which was attributed to the intramolecular and extramolecular hydrogen bonding. While the band 920–1290 cm−1 related to -OH bending and -C-O stretching was observed in the cross-linked polymer of PVA and CMC. Moreover, when I2 was compounded with PAI/CMC, the absorption peak at 2870 cm−1 of hydroxyl stretching reappeared changed. In the sample of PAI/CMC/CQDs, the huge broad peak at 3410 cm−1 and 820 cm−1 were attributed to -OH bending from alcoholysis reaction of PVA and CMC, the stretching vibration of the CN was at 1280 cm−1. The above results indicate that the specific interactions to form the hydrogels，.

The semi-crystalline structure of PVA chains was verified by XRD patterns, which indicated crystal diffraction with a strong or wide peak at 19.6° and 40.9°. PVA/CMC had a weaker crystal diffraction pattern than that of PVA [50], expressing that the original polymeric structures of CMC and PVA had altered (Fig. 1c). Furthermore, the addition of KI-I2 resulted in the appearance of a new broad peak at 6.6°, evidencing the formation of the In chain [51]. Besides, PAI/CMC/CQDs showed the stronger crystal diffraction pattern than that of PAI/CMC, which demonstrated a new polymeric structure appeared in PAI/CMC/CQDs.

In order to study the effect of CMC on water absorption of hydrogels, the swelling rate and water content of the PAI/CMC/CQDs with different components were compared (Fig. 1d and Fig. S4). CMC had minimal influence on the moisturizing property and boosted swelling rate of hydrogel. The higher swelling rate made the hydrogel more efficient in absorbing wound fluids. When the feeding ratio of PVA:CMC was 10:0.9, hydrogels showed excellent swelling rates and optimal material utilization. Through SEM analysis (Fig. 1e and Fig. S5), PAI/CMC/CQDs retained the spanning structural properties of the PVA hydrogel, and the web structure was more dense with most of the pores had a size of 17.5 ± 1.8 μm and a few of that were 37.5 ± 1.7 μm. The high interconnected porous architectures of hydrogel were suitable for gas and nutrient exchange, blood absorption, or wound exudates at the wound sites. To investigate the distribution of each component in the composite hydrogels, the EDS spectrum of PAI/CMC/CQDs was visible. The element distribution map of C, O, N, and Na (Fig. 1f) revealed that CMC, CQDs, and I2 were dispersed uniformly in the PAI/CMC/CQDs hydrogel [52].

The pH level is a crucial monitoring indication in wound healing, since it reflects the wound infection and wound reconstruction process [53]. We developed a visible light and fluorescent dual-color monitoring system to satisfy the demand for precise wound pH measurement. Firstly, we examined the fluorescence of CQDs in aqueous solutions of various pH levels (Fig. 2a). When the pH values increased from 5 to 8, the fluorescence absorption peak progressively moved from 525 nm to 459 nm, and the fluorescence color changed from vivid blue to brilliant yellow with a linear relationship, which was suitable to combine with PAI/CMC to synthesize the fluorescent hydrogels for monitoring pH values of wound (Fig. 2b and Fig. S7). It was easy to conduct firsthand observations. To evaluate the fluorescence change after the addition of CQDs to the hydrogel, we investigated the effect of pH values on the fluorescence performance of PAI/CMC/CQDs. As the pH climbed, the color of the hydrogels changed from blue to fluorescent yellow (Fig. 2c). Under the sunlight, the color of hydrogel was altered from to dark blue to yellow because of the presence of PAI (Fig. 2d). The pH-dependent color shift made the field wound status easily visible to the naked eye, and fluorescence properties and UV features were negatively correlated with pH values.

It is well known that almost all visible colors are superimposed by red, green, and blue (RGB), however, color identification by the naked eye is prone to inaccuracy. Therefore, the smartphone was applied to convert the images into remote RGB detection mode in order to obtain a correlation equation between wound pH and RGB signals. In turn, the wound treated with PAI/CAM/CQDs could be monitored remotely in real time. Via translating color change into RGB signals, the bicolorimetric accuracy was improved to some extent. The RGB data from the images were subsequently calibrated in terms of the RGB strength and pH value of the hydrogels. The signal was standardized by dividing each R value by the corresponding R + G + B value. As displayed in Fig. 2e, under UV light, when the pH increased, the R, G, and B values increased, the specific calculation formula was shown in Eq. (1). Under visible light (Fig. 2f), R, G, and B values improved with increasing pH values, the specific calculation formula was shown in Eq. (2). The above experimental results fully demonstrated that our design hydrogels were the ideal materials to implement personalized wound treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical properties and swelling behavior characterization
ce:section.ce:para: Good flexibility and mechanical robustness help the hydrogel dressings well fit the wounds on different parts of the body [54]. To this end, we characterized a series of mechanical properties of the hydrogel materials. Firstly, Fig. 3a-3d exhibited PAI/CMC/CQDs the exceptional stretchability and form adaptation, which was easy to stretch, bend and twist because of its weak modulus. Moreover, a tensile machine was used to test the mechanical properties of the PVA, PAI, PAI/CMC, PAI/CMC/CQDs. When the hydrogels were all compressed to 80 % strain, the compressive strength of PAI/CMC/CQDs increased to 250 kPa, which was significantly higher than that of PVA (150 kPa) (Fig. 3e). This was owing to the introduction of I2, CMC, and CQDs, that reduced the free volume of the macromolecules and formed stronger hydrogen bonds between the molecular chains. Besides, we investigated the cyclic compression characteristics of PAI/CMC/CQDs (Fig. 3f) for four cycles at varied compressive stresses (20 %, 40 %, 60 %, and 80 %). Notably, the hydrogels showed the sufficient elasticity to preserve good shape-variation adaptability, which could satisfy the requirements for compression properties in applications. Meanwhile, the stress-strain curves were utilized to analyze the mechanical performance of hydrogels. The findings revealed that the tensile strength was around 40 kPa and the strain at break was over 250 % (Fig. 3g). Clinically, hydrogel dressings with good tensile properties can adapt to the high-stretch application scenarios, and their tensile strain are generally within 150 % [55]. More surprisingly, the PAI/CMC/CQDs were stretched 10 times at 220 % strain with tiny hysteresis loops, suggesting their remarkable tensile recovery capacity (Fig. 3h).

At a frequency of 1 Hz, we measured the rheological properties of hydrogels exposed to stresses ranging from 0.1 % to 10.0 %. Compared to PVA, the energy storage modulus of PAI/CMC/CQDs was significantly larger and raised to 4.03 ± 0.42 kPa (Fig. 3i), which might be ascribed to the inclusion of I2, CMC, and CQDs to construct a denser physical cross-linked network inside PAI/CAM/CQDs. Overall, the hybrid hydrogels demonstrated mechanical stability and elastic shape recovery, demonstrating strong adaptability to various types of particular wounds, and improved the adhesion of the hydrogels to the wound area.

Furthermore, the swelling properties of the hydrogels were characterized to verify their ability to absorb water. PAI/MC/CQDs were submerged in PBS buffers at various pH values to explore the effect of pH on the swelling properties of the materials. As showed in Fig. 3j, the swelling ratio of PAI/MC/CQDs was greatly increased at pH = 8, which allowed the dressings to continuously absorb exudate from the wound tissues. The strong water absorption ability of PAI/MC/CQDs was due to the hydrophilic CMC and PVA in the hydrogel backbone, which absorbed far more water than its own weight, helping to quickly remove of exudate from chronic wounds and provide a moist healing environment.

Further, the degradability of the PAI/CMC/CQDs dressing in vitro was investigated within 5 days. As shown in Fig. S6, there was no obvious weight damage of PAI/CMC/CQDs in PBS solution without NIR, while a slight change occurred in the PAI/CMC/CQDs group with NIR, which might be due to the loosening of the porous structure by the high temperature environment (∼ 45 °C) from the photothermal conversion. In addition, after treating with lysozyme, the weight loss was only 2.3 % and 1.9 % in PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR group and PAI/CMC/CQDs group. This weaker enzymatic degradation performance was attributed to the partial hydrolysis of the glycosidic linkages of CMC in the PAI/CMC/CQDs hydrogel backbone by lysozyme [56]. Therefore, the well-stabilized PAI/CMC/CQDs hydrogel is a promising wound dressing.

Before the wound repair treatment, the photothermal performance of PAI/CMC/CQDs hydrogels was thoroughly evaluated in vitro. Firstly, we investigated the photothermal conversion effect of PVA, PVA/CMC, PAI/CMC, and PAI/CMC/CQDS hydrogels under NIR light irradiation. As seen in Fig. S8a and Fig. 4a, the surface temperatures of PAI/CMC/CQDs risen rapidly and reached a platform under NIR for 10 min, demonstrating that PAI/CMC/CQD hydrogel excellent photothermal activity. Whereafter, the photothermal conversion capability of PAI/CMC/CQDs hydrogels under incremental NIR laser power intensities was also examined (Fig. 4b). After the NIR light irradiation with laser densities of 0.5, 0.8, 1.0, and 1.2 W/cm2, the temperature of PAI/CMC/CQDs reached 32.7 °C, 37.9 °C, 44.9 °C, and 49.4 °C, respectively. It was determined that the higher temperature of roughly ∼45 °C was the threshold for skin scald, which was crucial for thermal wound healing reason [57]. Moreover, with the increase of I2 content (0, 50, 100, 150，200 and, 250 μg/mL), the temperature of PAI/CMC/CQDs rose to 45.1 °C (26.5, 28.7, 35, 41, 44.2 and, 45.1 °C) under the laser irradiation for 10 min (Fig. S 8b). It showed that the temperature elevation of PAI/CMC/CQDs was positively related to the concentration of PAI/CMC/CQDs and the photothermal conversion rate was up to 18 % (Fig. 4c). Furthermore, the photothermal stability of the PAI/CMC/CQDs was also evaluated. As demonstrated in Fig. 4d, the temperature of the PAI/CMC/CQDs remained ∼45 °C under irradiation 3 cycles with 808 nm laser, which indicated that the hydrogel material had good photothermal stability.

To further investigate the effect of NIR light on the release of antibacterial agents, the release rate of I2 from the hydrogel samples was tested with and without light radiation (1 W/cm2, 10 min). As displayed in Fig. 4e, the release rate of I2 under NIR was in the light group was significantly higher than that in the non-light group and demonstrated a “off-on” behavior triggered by NIR. This was because PAI/CMC/CQDs converted the absorbed NIR light into heat to accelerate the release of I2. The temperature sensitive PAI combined of PVA and polyiodide chain resulted in drug bursting to release from the cross-linked hydrogel network. Adequate amounts of the antimicrobial agent of I2 acting on inflammatory wounds would shorten the process of healing [58,59]. In view of the exceptional photothermal performance, photothermal conversion rate, and, photothermal responsive release capacity, PAI/CMC/CQDs was a powerful weapon to fight germs and prevent infection.

The in vitro cumulative release of CQDs from PAI/CMC/CQDs and PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR hydrogel were also explored in PBS with different pH at 37 °C. As shown in Fig. S9, the release amount of CQDs from PAI/CMC/CQDs hydrogels with or without NIR were positively correlated with pH values, which was due to the high swelling rate of PAI/CMC/CQDs hydrogels in alkaline environment. Furthermore, the release amount of CQDs from PAI/CMC/CQDs hydrogels with NIR at pH = 8 were no >4 μg, that of in the group without NIR was 4.5 μg. The negligible release of CQDs would ensure the fluorescent stability of PAI/CMC/CQDs for continuous wound monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial activity and hemostasis test of PAI/CMC/CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial effect of the hydrogel materials had been evaluated in vitro. As seen in Fig. 5a, the PVA/CMC hydrogel hardly affected the proliferation of E.coli (gram-negative bacterium) and S.aureus (gram-positive bacterium), while the addition of iodine improved the antibacterial activity of the material of PAI/CMC. To be observed, PAI/CMC/CQDs (200 μg/mL) partially suppressed the bacterial growth, and the higher bacterial inhibition efficiency was achieved under laser irradiation (1 W/cm2, 808 nm) due to the photothermal-responsive I2 release from PAI/CMC/CQDs. Furthermore, FESEM was utilized to demonstrate the antibacterial activity of the hydrogels. When S.aureus and E.coli were exposed to iodine-containing hydrogel, the morphology of bacteria changed to different degrees (Fig. 5b). When the bacteria were exposed to NIR (1 W/cm2, 808 nm), the rod-like structure of E. coli and the globular structure of S. aureus both suffered significant damage, with numerous surface cracks, indicating destroy to the bacterial membrane. Besides, the relative bacteria death rates of E.coli and S.aureus incubated with PAI hydrogel (200 μg/mL) were 92 % and 98.6 %, respectively. (Fig. 5c and Fig. 5d) These findings suggested that the PAI/CMC/CQDs with an effective light-activated antibacterial activity, successfully preventing bacteria multiplication.

To assess the hemostatic effect of the hydrogel, the blood cell coagulation effect of the PAI/CMC/CQDs hydrogel and PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR were measured using the mouse whole blood. The results were shown in Fig. S10, the blood without hydrogel remained in a free-flowing state. In contrast, when whole blood was mixed with PAI/CMC/CQDs hydrogel or PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR, the blood rapidly gelated. The gelation of blood was mainly attributed to the closed porous structure and swelling effect of PAI/CMC/CQDs hydrogels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro hemolysis rate and biocompatibility of PAI/CMC/CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The hemolysis rate of hydrogels was tested in vitro and the result was exhibited in Fig. S11, the hemolysis rates for PVA/CMC, PAI/CMC, PAI/CMC/CQDs, and PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR were 0.59 ± 0.14 %, 2.19 ± 0.14 %, 3.21 ± 0.23 %, and 2.78 ± 0.12 %, respectively. The hemolysis rates of the hydrogels in all experimental groups were below the safety threshold of 5.0 % for clinical application [60], indicating that PAI/CMC/CQDs have good hemocompatibility as the hydrogel dressings. Besides, favorable biocompatibility is an essential requirement for designing new biomedical materials [61]. Subsequently, L929 cells and HUVECs were treated with the hydrogel materials for 24 h, and the viability of cells was examined by the CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. 5e and Fig. S12a, the cell viability of HUVECs cells and L929 cells were higher than 80 % after co-incubating with different materials (VA/CMC, PAI/CMC, PAI/CMC/CQDs, and PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR) for 1 day, 3 days, or 5 days. According to ISO 10993-5:1999 [62], the corresponding cytotoxicity grade is 0–1, indicating that the formulated hydrogels were non-toxic. Even in the heat treatment group, the composite hydrogel displayed a higher cell viability, promoting the proliferation of L929 and HUVECs. Moreover, the biocompatibility was performed to of the PAI/CMC/CQDs was further evaluated by a live/dead staining assay. As exhibited in Fig. 5f and Fig. S12b, bright green fluorescence (labeling living cells) indicated that almost all cells survived in the control, PVA/CMC, PAI/CMC, and PAI/CMC/CQDs groups, confirming the excellent biocompatibility of PVA/CMC, PAI/CMC, and PAI/CMC/CQDs hydrogel. Furthermore, the green fluorescence intensity from living cells was stronger in the group of PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR compared to the other groups, suggesting that hydrogels under NIR might promote cell proliferation.

More importantly, the effect of various materials on cell migration was verified by the transwell invasion assay. Similarly, L929 cells and HUVECs were seeded in well plates and co-incubated with PVA/CMC, PAI/CMC, and PAI/CMC/CQDs hydrogel, and the experimental results were displayed in Fig. 5g. Distinctly, the moving speed of cells in PAI/CMC/CQDs-treated group after NIR illumination greatly accelerated compared to that of in other experimental groups. Consequently, the photo-activated exothermic PAI/CMC/CQDs was expected to simultaneously resistance to antimicrobial drugs and promote epithelial cells migration, wound healing might be hastened. Overall, the hydrogel dressing of PAI/CMC/CQDs with good cytocompatibility would be a promising candidate for clinical applications in wound healing [63].

Furthermore, the skin defect model was created to evaluate the effect of composite hydrogel dressings on wound healing in vivo (Fig. 6 ). We conducted a 14-day treatment according to the timeline (Fig. 6a). Firstly, the temperature changes at the wound area were initially captured by a thermal imager (Fig. 6b). After 10 min of NIR radiation, the wound surface temperature after material treatment steadily increased to about 45 °C, reflecting the controllable photothermal conversion ability of PAI/CMC/CQDs. As showed in Fig. 6c and Fig. 6d, the wound healing speed was faster in the PVA/CMC-treated group than that in the control group during the treatment course. This phenomenon might be related to the effective hygroscopic qualities of CMC [64]. At the same time, the hydrogel containing PAI had a greater healing rate than the hydrogel containing PVA, which attributed to the superior antibacterial activity of I2 for promoting healing. In addition, the wound healing process of PAI/CMC-treated group and PAI/CMC/CQDs-treated group was similar, indicating that the PAI/CMC/CQDs dressing had a certain therapeutic effect, but was not enough to cure chronic wounds. Distinctly, the wound of the mouse treated with PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR had better closed on day 14. As well, it could be calculated that the relative wound area in PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR group was always smaller than that of other groups in the same period (Fig. 6d), implying that the photothermal-induced release of I2 greatly enhanced the antibacterial effect to speed up wound repair.

Skin samples were taken from wounds and nearby tissues of each group of experimental mice and stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and Masson's trichrome on day 14 to measure wound healing and regeneration (Fig. 6e). Compared to the control group, the wounds in the PVA, PVA/CMC, PAI/CMC, and PAI/CMC/CQDs groups were covered with intact and continuous epidermis, and the newly formed epidermis in the PAI/CMC/CQDS + NIR group had a thickness more similar to that of normal tissue with maximum amount of mature blood vessels, hair follicles, thickened epidermis and well-proliferated fibroblast. Besides, collagen serves as the building block for new tissue during wound healing, the collagen content may be used as a measure of how well a wound has healed. The blue hue was deeper in the PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR group compared to that in PAI/CMC/CQDs group, which indicated that NIR stimulated rapid collagen production. Additionally, collagen fibers in the PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR group were compact, while those in the control group were loose indicated the new skin tissue was being formed under NIR. These substantial differences between the PAI/CMC/CQDs + NIR, PAI/CMC/CQDs, PAI/CMC, and PVA/CMC groups verified that NIR-inspired antibacterial hydrogel dressings significantly improved collagen deposition. These findings demonstrated that PAI/CMC/CQDs hydrogels could make wound healing and closure, when irradiated with NIR light, promoted wound healing and closure. On the one hand, the thermal-responsive antimicrobial agents release inhibited bacterial infections, on the other hand, the safe temperature thresholds (45 °C) promoted skin remodeling, this strategy combined to achieve rapid wound healing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time wound pH monitoring of PAI/CMC/CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The wound healing process of different patients is not the same, real-time pH monitoring of wounds would be conducive to the implementation of personalized treatment. Therefore, we assembled the pH-responsive hydrogel dressings and verified the pH sensitivity to the changes of pH values in vitro. Next, we investigated the pH monitoring capacity of PAI/CMC/CQDs in the wound healing process in vivo. As displayed in Fig. 7a, from day 0 to day 3, the color of the hydrogel changed under either visible or UV light, which corresponded to the change in wound pH. The initial pH value of injured tissues in mice was 5.3 due to the acidic environment caused by inflammation. After that, the wound pH value increased significantly from 5.3 to 7.3 on day 2, the possible cause was the influence of local bodily fluids (back to 7.4). The wound entered the healing phase on the third day, and the pH progressively returned to normal (Fig. 7b). Notably, the pH test paper was employed to measure the true pH values of mouse wound tissue fluid at different time points, and the change trend was almost completely consistent with the results calculated by the two-color change of hydrogel, indicating that PAI/CMC/CQDs had accurate diagnostic ability.

In summary, a multifunctional wound healing hydrogel with dual colorimetric detecting function to promote wound healing and monitor wound status was prepared by polyvinyl alcohol, sodium carboxymethylcellulose, iodine, and carbon quantum dots. The hydrogel with various functions including excellent biocompatibility, antibacterial and hemostatic, which have been experimentally proved as a promising wound dressing in this work. Moreover, the hydrogel with a good photothermal effect, which not only improved the antibacterial activity, but promoted epidermis cell migration to speed up wound healing. The monitoring system mediated by PAI and CQDs was used in conjunction with a smartphone to directly read the pH of the wounded tissue, allowing visualization of the healing status of the wound and scientific guidance on dressing change frequency. Above all, the PAI/CMC/CQDs dressing provided a new method for accelerating bacterial death, would healing and feedbacking wound status, and is expected to enable personalized care and precise treatment of chronic wounds in clinic.
